text: This will be a discovery research interview, and the questions will be open-ended.
There are no right or wrong answers, so answer as honestly as you can.
Are you ready to get started?
Yeah.
When you're getting ready to run a round of moderated research interviews, what's the very first thing you do to start organizing the recruitment process?
I normally come up with a plan for the types of people I want to recruit, normally as an audience definition paragraph.
And then from there, I work out the types of things I would ask to get that.
To be honest, I mostly use AI in this to split it out as a starting point.
I always need to tweak it as well to make sure it works within Askable's system.
But it is normally a few back and forths.
And then once I have those, I'll put the criteria in.
Could you tell me more about that initial audience definition paragraph you create?
It's pretty high level.
It's just like, who are the types of people who I need to talk to in this moderated interview?

So for me here at Askable, I will be trying to find either clients or people who would use Askable, such as designers, Researchers, product managers, people like that, usually just trying to work out if there's a specific niche within that.
So it might be like, oh, who are people who use AI in X part of their workflow?
Walk me through a specific time when you were defining one of those niches, like the AI workflow example you mentioned.

Recently, I was trying to find people in the US who, I guess, actually, they work on products and they make decisions regularly based on very little customer data, but they understand the value of data.
They know it's wrong when they're not, and they copy competitors blindly and have felt the issues of shipping something that doesn't have the impact they expected.
How did it feel trying to find that specific group of people who are blindly copying competitors and lacking data?
It felt hard because especially our panel doesn't have too many people who are professionals because they'd never get chosen for any opportunities.
So especially like if they work in UX or design or research.
So it's quite difficult to find them and I lack trust because in the US I feel like anyone who does fit the criteria well is probably a scammer.
So yeah, it's pretty hard.
What was a different recruitment scenario where you felt more confident about the quality of the participants you were looking for?
If I'm trying to find people not for our specific use case.
So, for example, with an industry stream, we can do some fairly tight screening, which isn't obvious as to what the correct response is.
And it's good for like that gen pop sort of recruit.
Could you explain the specific steps you take to design those tight screening questions that make it hard for someone to guess the correct answer?
Yeah, so I'll create questions with a broad range of answers.
I'll only screen in the very specific answers.
I'll put in options that seem like they're the right answer, just in the way they're written.
I will also, most recently, I also choose to handpick them after the fact.
I ask them for their LinkedIn sometimes.
What else?
I sometimes put in trick answers for those who just select all options to try and get eligible.
Yeah, that's probably it.
When you decide to handpick participants after they've passed your screeners, What does your decision-making process look like as you review their profiles?
I mostly look at what their job and industry is on their core profile versus what they said it is after the fact.
Sometimes if I ask for their LinkedIn, I will look at their LinkedIn and make sure the names match up.
I look at the fact that their LinkedIn looks real as well and not just created as a shell for these sorts of things.
I sometimes ask for where they work and what their role is and try and find them on LinkedIn if I don't ask that.
Yeah, I've got a few different tactics there, but it's still difficult to know.
What prompts you to go through the extra effort of searching for participants on LinkedIn versus just relying on their screener responses?
If they're a professional, I pretty much always use LinkedIn in some way.
If they're more, if it's just gen pop, like you'd, typically get a good range of people.
And you can ask screeners in a way that isn't obvious what the proper answer, like what the eligible answers are.
[AI Actions Layer] What specific details or red flags are you looking for when you compare their screener answers to their actual LinkedIn profile?
[Speaker:Brandon Di Bartolo] I look at name, places they worked, their industry, their role.
That's pretty much it.
Nothing too much.
Split-second thought.
The participant has explained their process audience definition AI/Askable screener setup LinkedIn verification.
Provided 2 examples: US decision-makers, without data, and industry-specific gen pop.
Shared their motivations: avoiding scammers, ensuring professional authenticity, and mentioned the feeling of it being odd or difficult.
I have position, reasoning, 2 examples, motivations, and emotional/contextual grounding.
Thank you.
Those details about how you verify professional backgrounds on LinkedIn are very helpful for understanding your planning process.
Since we are reaching the end of our time, I have one final question.
How do you typically decide how many participants from each of those defined niches to include in your interview schedule?
Normally I'll set that up front of like what the quota is.
So depending on the type of research I'm doing, I'll have some guidelines of what I feel is the right amount to talk to.
You know, if it's an unmoderated study and it's not that important, I'll talk to about 20.
If it's moderated, probably 5 to 10.
If it's a survey, I'll push it up to about 500.
Like, it varies depending on the, I guess, how the potential impact to getting it wrong versus the importance of, or how much we already know as well.
Thank you so much for sharing those insights.
Understanding how you balance sample size against the impact of the research is very helpful.
Next, we are going to transition into a website usability test where I will ask you to share your screen.
I'll provide some specific instructions for a task, and then we will open the website in your browser to see how you navigate it.
Could you please click the screen sharing button and choose the option to share this window?
I've got your screen share now.
Thank you.
For this next part, I'd like you to create a new interview study in Askable.
Set up the study as though you are conducting discovery research about TV streaming services.
I'm going to open the website for you now.
As you go through this, please think out loud and describe what you're doing and what's on your mind.
As you get started with setting up that interview study for TV streaming research, Please remember to think aloud so I can follow your process.
Just let me know when you feel you've finished the task.
Okay, great.
I'll get started here.
So I am going to start by creating a new study.
So I'm going to click over here and I'm going to create an interview study because I believe that's what we're talking about.
Can you just remind me what the study was about again?
[AI Actions Layer] It's discovery research about TV streaming services.
So I'm typing TV streaming discovery.
Okay, so the next thing I'm going to do is create that.
For this one, I'm going to do a video call because I want it to be moderated.
I'm going to go next here.
For device, because it's discovery and I'm not going to expect them to share their screen, or I'm not going to do it, I don't have specific requirements.
So I'm going to click on any device here.
For the tool, I'm going to use Askable as well.
There are some other options behind here, but the they all suck.
Okay, so for the amount of people this week, I'm pretty time poor, so I'm just going to go with 5.
But depending on what I'm trying to learn, that would go up.
I probably wouldn't ever do less than 5 though, so let's go with that.
Focus groups for the week, we're going to be doing individual interviews.
And 45 minutes is good.
Typically, I try and aim for half an hour, but a bit of buffer time there as well.
As I mentioned before, I'm probably going to handpick this one because it's very much for moderated.
You don't want to end up with the wrong participants.
Automated gets you a nice little— you get some efficiencies out of that, but especially when it comes to scheduling and stuff.
No one wants to be doing that, but handpicking for this is going to work, I think.
In this case, it's pretty gen pop, so it's a broad consumer base.
Let's go Askable panel.
Big focus on the US at the moment, so let's do that United States as our option, and let's remove Australia.
Cool.
For demographic filtering, I don't often use this.
I don't like to filter up front.
I'd rather do the filtering in the handpicking.
So I don't want to block anyone from being able to come through.
And I haven't spoken to anyone recently around this topic, so I'm going to not exclude anyone.
So if it was in a similar topic I've spoken to, I'd do at least a 3-month exclusion.
Okay, so gonna want some screeners here.
So we'll start with some multi-choice.
Go, which best describes your habits or how you interact?
This is a terrible screener and I can't type while I'm talking.
So, with TV streaming services.
So, first option is I don't use them at all.
I use them a few times per week.
I use them daily.
I use them multiple times per day.
We're just going to make all these ineligible and just keep that one.
Cool.
Next up, we might— oh, that was a poor experience.
I needed to click this button.
Cool.
Oh, that's not what I want to do.
Let's go.
What is the name of your favorite TV show that you're watching right now?
Save.
Which TV streaming service are you using right now?
Okay, and then we're going to add Netflix and Disney Plus.
Let's make these ineligible and then go— what else have we got?
Prime Video.
Let's just stick with those.
Okay, save that.
Now that I've got those screeners, I'm probably going to get some poor results from these, though the screening is pretty tight and people won't know how to guess that.
I am handpicking as well., but this is general consumer, so it's usually okay.
Additional information, I am in Brisbane.
Um, so do you watch TV, um, on streaming services?
Um, cool, that's fine.
Next.
Um, I don't have any additional instructions for them.
Um, I am the person running the research and I'm going to use Askable standard.
I'm going to lock in some times.
So I typically avoid lunchtime.
So let's make sure we got 9 AM, leave a bit of a gap in between some of these, and then I normally create enough for some backup slots just over the next 2 days.
So we want to get 5 across those.
Okay, now I'm going through and I'm going to submit that study.
And that's it.
Thank you for working through that.
Shall we move on to the next part?
Yeah, let's do it.
Perfect.
Thank you.
Are you ready?
Can you move on to the next part?
When you were setting up that interview study in Askable just now, what specifically stood out as the most frustrating part of the process?
I think the setting up of the screeners is a little bit clunky.
I find it a bit difficult sometimes, particularly when there's certain options I would hard screen out and then there's certain options where I may select them.
I think the other thing is I hate setting up that calendar.
I wish I could just send them a link to my calendar availability and then they can lock in slots with me after I select them.
So rather than me putting them in, they applying for times that they're available.
I'd rather focus on picking the right person rather than picking the most, um, the best time.
Um, I would focus on a better participant over a convenient time.
Could you walk me through the specific steps you took when the screener felt clunky to you?
Um, mostly around, um, The screener felt clunky.
I think the screener felt clunky, particularly I clicked that big black button instead of the text button to add another screener.
I think it just felt wrong.
And then, yeah, I feel like there was a few options missing from what I would expect as well.
What specific options did you expect to see in the screener that were missing?
Like what I said before, the may select, will select, screen out, those sorts of options.
How did not having those may select or will select options specifically impact the time it took you to finish the setup?
It doesn't affect the setup.
In fact, it's more options, but it will help me with the next part, which is the actual screening when I'm handpicking.
How does the prospect of having to handpick participants without those specific labels change your mood about the upcoming analysis phase?
It just makes it slightly harder.
Like, it's not the analysis phase, it's more when I choose them to lock them in.
When you've had to handpick participants in the past, Without those specific May select filters, how has that extra effort impacted your overall project timeline?
I don't know.
I can't call on a recent example that would be accurate.
Thinking about the calendar setup you mentioned earlier, could you describe a time when manually entering those slots felt particularly burdensome?
Yeah, so sometimes a while back I was doing continuous discovery research every week.
Where I would have like a day of slots every single week and I had to set it up for, you know, 6 months worth.
So I went and had to manually create individual blocks in all of those like different time slots every Thursday for every week for 6 months.
And it was a nightmare.
How did spending all that time manually entering those calendar blocks for 6 months affect your motivation to keep up with the project?
I mean, it was just in the setup.
Once it was set up, it was fine.
But it definitely made me not want to set up another one after the fact.
Aside from the calendar and the screener buttons, what other part of the study creation process feels like it slows you down the most?
I think the fact that every step is just a whole page.
And it feels like you have to go through more steps than there actually are.
If it were all kind of on one page with just sections, it would feel like it's less overwhelming.
When you encounter that page-by-page layout, what specifically about it makes the process feel overwhelming for you?
I think the fact that things get added in and you don't really realize, so it's not a true indication of progress.
I think also it just feels clunky, like it doesn't feel like it's part of the same experience.
It feels very much step, step, step, step, and I don't know, makes it feel a bit yuck.
Additionally, I think with the— when you— if you— I've noticed this before— if you change something on some pages and don't click the next button, it doesn't save.
It's particularly a problem when you go back and change something and you don't expect you have to click next because you're not trying to get to the next block.
You try and skip back to the one you were at.
Can you tell me about a specific time when you lost work or had to redo a section because you forgot to click the next button to save?
I don't remember a specific time, but there was one where I submitted it with the wrong quota And that's because I moved on without realizing that the quota wasn't at the updated limit.
And then I wasn't able to fix this after the fact.
I had to go and request it from an administrator, I guess, customer success person.
Thank you so much for sharing your thoughts and walking me through your process today.
Your feedback on the recruitment and setup experience has been incredibly valuable.
